title: As Long as It Is Today
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: As Long as it is Today - Pastor Tito Lyro - Hebrews 3_12-15.txt

outline.title: Introduction: What We Do on Sundays
subpoints: The people of God gathering together to worship is a celebration of union with Christ and with one another—not merely a religious routine
By the simple virtue of being together in worship, we are proclaiming the gospel of Jesus Christ and glorifying Christ as our head
Every single Lord's Day provides the opportunity to remind ourselves how important life in community is for followers of Jesus Christ
The warning of Hebrews 3:12-15 is given not to outsiders but to those who profess faith—'the brethren' and 'partakers of Christ' (vv. 12, 14)

outline.title: The Seriousness of the Warning (v. 12)
subpoints: 'Beware' (v. 12) is a very strong word: take care, see to it, take heed—all communicate urgency and seriousness
The consequence of not listening is a hardened heart—to disobey the voice of God and act according to one's own desire
This warning describes the progressive steps by which a Christian's heart can reach the point of actually turning away from Christ
What we read here is a picture of what sin does if undetected, unexposed, and unforsaken

outline.title: Step 1: Giving Way to Sin in My Life
subpoints: It begins with letting things into my life outside the boundaries of what God calls me to be and do—things that God Himself calls evil
When conviction comes through conscience and the Holy Spirit, I face a crucial choice: confess and rest in Christ's mercy, or erect a system of self-justification
When I choose self-justification, I am making myself feel good about what God says is not good—participating in my own spiritual blindness
Paul Tripp: the pastor who was just angry in the elders' meeting will tell himself he was just speaking like one of God's prophets; the gossip will tell herself it was an extended prayer request

outline.title: Step 2: Unbelief (v. 12)
subpoints: If we are not aware of our ability to deceive ourselves and create self-justifying systems, the next step is unbelief
Rather than simple faith and rest in the accurate diagnosis of God's Word, we work to convince ourselves that we are not in fact sinners in need of mercy
We can say we are sinners in the abstract, but we are very good at explaining away particular instances of sin as not actually being sinful
The seed of every sin dwells in every heart—we are all capable of the most heinous sins; only the grace of God restrains us

outline.title: Step 3: Departing from the Living God (v. 12)
subpoints: What anchors us against the storms of temptation is a firm acceptance of what the Bible says about our sins and firm rest in the grace of Christ
When we cut that rope through self-justification and unbelief, we drift further from Christ—our natural tendency is not toward Christ but away from Him
Like the Spanish Armada that used up all its anchors and was destroyed by a hurricane without one battle, losing our anchors in the Word leaves us adrift
The word 'departing' (v. 12) is related to apostasy—falling away from faith

outline.title: Step 4: A Hardened Heart (v. 13)
subpoints: What once bothered us doesn't bother us anymore; what once activated our conscience no longer does; what we knew was outside God's boundaries now lives comfortably inside our boundaries
Paul calls this a 'seared conscience'—a conscience that may still be yelling and screaming but is blocked and no longer heard
A hard heart is a stony heart: not malleable, not shaped by the Spirit, hardened and resistant to change—a reversal of the new covenant promise of a heart of flesh
This is the most dangerous place for anyone who professes Christ to be

outline.title: Why Sin Deceives: Spiritual Blindness
subpoints: Sin deceives—and this is how a Christian can fall into this progression: we are not only blind, we are blind to our blindness
The spiritually blind person walks around with the delusion that no one sees himself more clearly than he does—and yet he is unaware of the most important things in his heart
David prayed in Psalm 139 that God would reveal the secret sins of his heart precisely because we cannot see them ourselves
Every person alive with remaining sin is a skilled self-deceiver—including pastors, including elders, including you and me

outline.title: The Essential Call: Daily Mutual Exhortation (v. 13)
subpoints: 'Exhort one another daily while it is called today'—the blinding ability of sin is so powerful that we literally need daily intervention from others
This passage crushes any allegiance to a 'just me and Jesus' Christianity; the Holy Spirit is arguing for the essentiality of the body of Christ in every Christian's life
Personal spiritual insight is a product of community: you cannot know yourself clearly apart from others in the body of Christ
Self-examination is a community project—Christians who convince themselves they can live outside of God's regular system of help are in danger of progressive spiritual blindness

outline.title: Getting to the End Is a Community Project
subpoints: Parents: if you don't love the church of Jesus Christ, your children will not love Christ—you cannot love Jesus apart from loving His people
God did not leave us to go through this life alone; He brought us into His church so that together we would grow in faith, each one a gift of grace to the others
Running the race is not a relay but a 'blob'—we are going together, sometimes carrying others, sometimes being carried

The church of Jesus Christ: exhort each other in the grace of Jesus Christ as long as it is Today; do not refuse people's presence in your life, for that is the means God has appointed for you to persevere to the end

summary:
This sermon on Hebrews 3:12-15 opens with a reminder that Sunday worship is not a mere religious routine but a gospel proclamation—the people of God gathered in union with Christ and one another, strengthening the body and glorifying the Head. Pastor Tito Lyro then turns to the sobering warning of the passage, which is directed not at outsiders but at those who profess faith in Christ, described as 'the brethren' and 'partakers of Christ.'

The heart of the sermon traces the four progressive steps by which a Christian's heart can harden toward Christ: first, giving way to sin and facing the choice between confession and self-justification; second, unbelief—convincing oneself that particular sins are not really sinful; third, departing from the living God as the anchors of God's Word are cut and the soul drifts away; and fourth, a fully hardened heart, no longer bothered by what once activated the conscience. Pastor Lyro illustrates each step vividly, drawing on Paul Tripp's examples of pastors and gossips rationalizing their sin, and the image of the Spanish Armada losing its anchors and being destroyed by a hurricane without a battle.

The sermon then explains why this progression is possible even for genuine believers: sin is fundamentally deceptive, and we are not only spiritually blind but blind to our blindness. This leads to the essential call of verse 13—daily mutual exhortation. The passage crushes any 'just me and Jesus' Christianity; the Holy Spirit argues that the body of Christ is essential to every believer's spiritual survival. Personal spiritual insight is a community project. The sermon closes with an urgent call: as long as it is Today, do not refuse the uncomfortable presence of others in your life, for that is the means God has appointed for perseverance to the end.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Hebrews 3:12-15

Beware, brethren, lest there be in any of you an evil heart of unbelief in departing from the living God; but exhort one another daily, while it is called "Today," lest any of you be hardened through the deceitfulness of sin. For we have become partakers of Christ if we hold the beginning of our confidence steadfast to the end, while it is said: "Today, if you will hear His voice, do not harden your hearts as in the rebellion."

## Sermon

### Introduction: The Significance of Gathering Together

What we do on Sundays is a very special thing. The people of God coming together to worship Father, Son, and Holy Spirit as the body of Christ is a celebration of our union with Christ and with one another. We are not alone in this world. We are united to the brethren, because of our head, the Lord Jesus Christ. This celebration of our mystical union with Christ and one another is a gospel celebration. Every time we gather together, by the simple virtue of being together and worshiping the Father through the Son by the Spirit, we are proclaiming the gospel of Jesus Christ that has united us to Him.

As people willingly commit to each other in worship in a covenantal way, the body of Christ is strengthened, the gospel is illustrated, and Christ is glorified. Every single Lord's Day provides us with the opportunity to remind ourselves how important life in community is for the followers of Jesus Christ.

Paul Tripp, in his book on pastoral ministry, says: "This passage puts before us a critical warning and an essential call that together reinforce the presence and power of remaining sin and the need for the daily ministry of the body of Christ in the life of every member—pastors included—of the body of Christ."

This warning is given to those who profess faith in Jesus Christ. This is not about people out there—it is about us in here. In verse 12 he talks about "the brethren." In verse 14 he describes the recipients as "partakers of Christ." The danger of hardening one's heart to Jesus Christ is a very real danger for those who believe in Jesus Christ.

### The Seriousness of the Warning (v. 12)

'Beware' at the beginning of verse 12 is a very strong word. The ESV says 'take care,' the NASB says 'see to it,' the old King James says 'take heed.' All communicate the seriousness of this warning given to the church of Jesus Christ.

The seriousness is also seen in the consequence of not listening: in verse 15, if you don't listen, your heart will be hardened—you will disobey the voice of God and act according to your own desires. When I say the voice of God, I am not talking about some mystical experience; I am talking about this book, the Bible. That is what we need to listen to.

This warning describes the progressive steps by which a Christian's heart can reach the point of actually turning away from Christ. What we read here is a picture of what sin does if undetected, unexposed, and unforsaken.

### Step 1: Giving Way to Sin

It all begins with me giving way to sin in my life. I let things into my life outside the boundaries of what God calls me to be and do—things God Himself calls evil. Because I am a believer with a heart of flesh rather than stone, my conscience bothers me and the Holy Spirit mercifully convicts me. At that point I face a crucial choice.

I can admit that what I have done is wrong and place myself once again under the mercies of Christ, receiving His forgiveness. Or I can erect a system of self-atonement and self-justification that argues for the rightness of what I have done. When I choose the second path, all I am doing is making myself feel good about what God says is not good. And that is a very dangerous place to be.

Every person still living with sin inside is a very skilled self-deceiver. Paul Tripp gives examples: The pastor who just became angry during an elder's meeting will tell himself he wasn't angry—he was just speaking like one of God's prophets. Wives who are gossiping about someone in their small group will tell themselves all the way home that it wasn't gossip, it was just an extended prayer request. The tight-fisted businessman who struggles to give will tell himself he is just being a good steward. Tripp concludes: "We all have a perverse ability to make ourselves feel good about what is in no way good."

### Step 2: Unbelief (v. 12)

If we are not aware of our ability to deceive ourselves, the next step comes in: unbelief. Rather than simple faith and rest in the accurate diagnosis of God's Word and the sufficient grace of Christ, we work to tell ourselves that we are not really sinners in need of forgiving mercy because what we have done is not actually wrong.

We are willing to say that we are sinners in general, but we are very good at trying to explain particular instances of sin as not being sinful. Let me challenge you: if all you can say is that you are a sinner in the abstract but you cannot actually name your sins, you may actually believe that you don't sin—because sin happens in real life, not in the abstract.

Remember what Robert Murray M'Cheyne said: the seed of every sin dwells in my heart. That is true of all of us. We are all capable of doing the most heinous sins. It is only the grace of God that restrains us from committing them. Never arrive at the place where you say "I could never do that"—it is the grace of God that keeps you from doing it.

### Step 3: Departing from the Living God (v. 12)

The word translated "departing" in verse 12 is related to the word apostasy and means to fall away. What anchors us against the storms of temptation is a firm acceptance of what the Bible says concerning our sins and a firm rest in the grace of Christ.

At camp this past summer we studied providence, including the Spanish Armada. One reason the Spanish Armada was defeated is that they had used all their anchors earlier in the campaign. When they were struck by the greatest northern hurricane ever recorded, they didn't have enough anchors to hold them in place, and they were blown off course. Most casualties happened there, without one bullet being fired. That is what happens when we cut the rope of what the Bible says concerning who we are and our sins—we drift further from Christ. Our natural tendency is not to drift toward Christ; it is to drift further away.

### Step 4: A Hardened Heart (v. 13)

What once bothered us doesn't bother us anymore. What once activated our conscience doesn't seem to activate it anymore. What we knew was outside God's boundaries now lives within our boundaries and doesn't matter to us anymore. This is what Paul calls having a seared conscience—a conscience that may still be speaking but that is blocked and no longer heard.

A hard heart is a stony heart. It is not malleable, cannot be shaped, is hardened and resistant to change—no longer tender and responsive to the squeeze of the Spirit's hands. This reverses the new covenant promise: the promise of the new covenant is a new heart of flesh. A hardened heart is going back toward a heart of stone.

There is no more dangerous place for one who professes Christ.

### Why Sin Deceives: We Are Blind to Our Blindness

How can these scary steps of hardening take place in the life of a Christian? Because sin is fundamentally deceptive. Who does sin first deceive? Me. I have no difficulty recognizing your sin. But I can be quite unprepared when my sin is pointed out.

Furthermore, spiritual blindness is not like physical blindness. When you are physically blind, you know you are blind, and you find ways to compensate. But spiritually blind people are not only blind—they are blind to their blindness. That is why David prayed in Psalm 139 that God would reveal to him the secret sins of his heart. The spiritually blind person walks around with the delusion that no one sees himself more clearly than he does—yet he is unaware of the most important things in his heart.

### The Essential Call: Daily Mutual Exhortation (v. 13)

Because we are blind to our own sin, the Holy Spirit gives us this essential call: "Exhort one another daily while it is called Today." Here is why this call is essential: the blinding ability of sin is so powerful and persuasive that you and I literally need daily intervention.

This passage is crushing any allegiance we might have to an isolated, individualized 'me and Jesus' Christianity. That doesn't exist—that leads to unbelief, and to hardening. The Holy Spirit is arguing for the essentiality of the ministry of others in the life of every Christian. None of us is wise enough to live this Christian life alone. None of us is safe living separated and unknown. Each of us—pastor or congregant—needs the eyes of others in order to see ourselves with clarity and accuracy. And that is uncomfortable.

Personal spiritual insight is a product of community—you cannot know yourself apart from others in the body of Christ. Every Christian needs people in his life in order to see himself with biblical accuracy. If you isolate yourself, you will remain in your blindness and in your false perception of yourself.

Parents: if you don't love the church of Jesus Christ, your children will not love Christ. You cannot love Jesus apart from loving His people.

### Getting to the End Is a Community Project

God in His infinite mercy and grace did not leave us to go through this life alone. He brought us into His church so that together we would grow in faith. Each one of us is a gift of grace to the others—remember 1 Corinthians 12, where every part of the body is necessary for the good of the whole body. To ignore our spiritual need for one another is to set out on the path that ends in a hardened heart toward Jesus Christ.

Running the race is not quite a relay because in a relay it is one after the other. It is more like a blob—we are going together. Sometimes people are in the back getting carried. Sometimes people in the front are carrying others. But we need that. Without that we will not get to the end.

So church of Jesus Christ, let us exhort each other in the grace of Jesus Christ, as long as it is Today. Do not refuse people's presence in your life, as uncomfortable as it might be. That is the means God has appointed for you and for me to persevere to the end. Beware, brethren, lest there be in any of you an evil heart of unbelief departing from the living God—but exhort one another daily while it is called Today.
